title: Bird Sound Identification Research
subtitle: Passive acoustic monitoring for avian conservation in Indonesia and Malaysia
reflection: I learned to fine-tune an established model (BirdNET) rather than training from scratch. The question is not always 'can I build this.' Sometimes it is 'should I.'
Spectrograms taught me that audio and vision pipelines are structurally similar. Sound is just images of time.
Running a research team means knowing when to step in and when to wait. I got that wrong more than once before I got it right.
I presented in front of ornithologists and signal processing researchers. Knowing the room matters as much as knowing the paper.

highlights: Took over as research lead. First task: build the dataset from scratch using Xeno-canto recordings.
Scoped to species endemic or endangered in Indonesia, Malaysia, and Borneo.
Built the dataset from audio recordings and negative samples (noise, speech, silence). Preprocessing: log-mel spectrograms, 96x511 px per clip.
Stratified split by species. No data leakage: split at recording level.
Fine-tuned BirdNET v2.4 on the endemic/endangered subset. Significant accuracy and F1 improvement over baseline.
Tested the model in the field: Bandung Zoo (Indonesia) and Malaysia National Zoo. Real animals, background noise, no controlled conditions.
Presented at ICITACEE 2025 in Semarang. Paper accepted and published in IEEE Xplore.

links.label: Paper: Bird Sound Identification in Indonesia and Malaysia (IEEE)

highlights: Working with the lecturers to extend the paper for PeerJ submission.
Adding MixIT source separation, unsupervised, no clean reference signal needed. Targets overlapping bird calls in noisy recordings.
The hard problem: two birds calling at once over wind and insects. MixIT handles this without labeled pairs.
